title: How He Vibe Coded His Way To Y Combinator

segments.text: This is Ismile and he got into Y
Combinator with a product built entirely
on Lowable 2,000 prompts, zero
traditional code, one month of work and
Ismile is not a developer, but that did
not stop him from building Brickwise, an
AI property manager that automate
maintenance for landlords and it's
already making revenue. In this video,
we're going to cover how he validated
and came up with the idea, his go-to
market strategy, and general advice that
he has for founders building with AI out
there. Let's jump into the video.
>> [music]
>> I'm here with this man and he went
absolutely viral on Twitter because he
claimed that he built his entire company
on lovable and got into YC. So the my
first question is is this true? At the
point of hitting the submit button on
our YC application. Yes, I have the logs
on lovable to prove it. We have a bunch
of uh prompts that we've paid for.
Having said that, my co-founder is one
of the most technical people in the
world and I'm pretty sure YC were very
well aware of that as a theme. Um, you
know, his previous company that he
worked in as an early engineer got
acquired by Google. He was one of four
engineers that worked on a product that
generated I think over $und00 million in
in revenue over a few years. He's an
extremely technical person. The reason
why the article had that theme is for us
what was more important than the quality
of what was being built at the time was
being able to identify a problem worth
solving. And I'm of the view that you
don't always need to spend a long time
writing highquality code to figure out
whether you're building something people
want. I think we were able to identify
that without that and we've since you
know built on the foundation of what we
first applied with.
>> How did you come up with the idea and
what is your story? I've had some
experience within kind of the the family
business dabbling into property and and
understanding what works and what
doesn't work and things like that. So I
had an understanding of the problem in
the property space and just
coincidentally at that time I think
lovable became visible. I'm sure it
existed slightly before that but okay
this thing seems quite cool. Let me just
play around with it. I think what
lovable have done has done sorry very
well is delivering what I described as
the magic moment very quickly. So the
fact that a nontechnical person can
write nontechnical words and produce a
technical outcome very quickly that's
like a huh this is really cool let me
keep going and uh before you guys took
away the uh one prompt per token charge
I used to write like you know massive
like five paragraph prompts to try and
maximize value I was probably the reason
you changed your system right [laughter]
but it it was really cool because I
could write stuff I could get outcomes I
could write stuff I could get outcomes
and I actually I think became reasonably
technical Google as I was figuring this
out. So I was creating edge functions on
supervase by speaking to chat GPT and
figuring out what that needs to look
like and then going back to loveboard
and getting it to talk to these systems.
But a lot of what we' done integrating
Stripe checkout, integrating Google
Analytics, Mix Panel, all of these
things were just back and forth
conversations between between myself and
and lovable.
>> Can you quickly describe to the viewers
uh what your company is or what your
product does?
>> Yes, probably should have started off
with that. We're building an AI property
manager that automates maintenance for
landlords and real estate agents.
There's two sides to the experience. The
tenant is given a phone number and an
email address. They can call, message,
WhatsApp 24/7. And the idea here is that
if there's a leak at 8:00 p.m.,
somebody's there to not just pick up the
call and help you fix it, but if it
can't fix the issue, it would contact
the maintenance teams from that landlord
or from that property management firm to
ensure that the issue is logged, that
there's a contractor going out. it'll
allow you to schedule with them and
things like that. And then of course on
the other side for the property manager
or you know for the for the landlord all
of these conversations are logged
recorded and they're given visibility to
ensure that things are being managed
properly.
>> So how would that go? Who would get the
product? Like who would be the paying
customer?
>> Maybe the landlord or the property
management firm and typically the
latter. Uh they're often managing one to
500 properties at the moment. Some are a
bit larger than that, some are a little
bit smaller, and they tend to have a
bottleneck in just how much demand that
they're getting from dealing with
issues. And that limits their ability to
scale. So, if you've got 200 properties
today and you want to get to 400, you
usually need to hire, you know, 1.7x the
staff members you have. That's often
quite difficult because it's quite
expensive up front and you might not
have the cash flow to do that. And so,
you need more properties to make the
money, but you can't get the money until
you get the properties. And so, you
can't hire the people to to do that. So,
there are all of these kind of back and
forth pieces. And I think AI has finally
made it possible to scale without having
to hire as quickly. So rather than 1.7x
the staff to 2x your output, you can
hire 1.2x the staff. So it might just be
one more person and then the the gap can
be filled by the product that we built.
>> And how many customers do you guys have
today?
>> I can't give the exact number, but we
kind of built out more technically
complex aspects of the product kind of
from getting into YC. uh in October we
were able to sign our first kind of
small landlords and then from the back
of that we started focusing on uh larger
contracts which we're in the process of
signing now as we build up to demo day
>> I think the viewers are going to be very
very curious to kind of uh the
integrations you have you have a like
various agents that are automating uh
different things I would assume
>> the agent right now can respond to
emails respond to WhatsApp respond to
SMS and uh pick up and respond to phone
calls once that happens that entire
conversation is logged We would then
find contractors that can fix that issue
for you. If the landlord or the property
manager doesn't have their preferred
contractors and we've plugged in a
Google API to be able to do this, we
filter for quality, locality, things
like that. And the idea here is that we
can pretty much guarantee that we'll
find someone available to fix the issue
for you right away. If you are a larger
company and you've got preferred or
full-time staff members to deal with
maintenance, we will contact them on
your behalf. The beauty of um AI is its
ability to do multiple things at the
same time. The greatest property manager
can still only really make one phone
call at a time, right? So, if it's
desperate, it's, you know, coming up to
Christmas, hardly anybody's available,
and it's cold, the heating is broken,
you know, frantically calling one at a
time until somebody picks up and
confirms that they're available. In the
case of AI, it can contact, you know, 50
at a time. And the beauty of that is you
can very quickly get the quote that you
need from of those 50, here's 10 quotes,
these seven out of budget, these three
are available today. Do you want to go
ahead and authorize that? The other part
of that is its ability to understand a
lot of context. So the best of property
managers are ones that actually know how
of property. Like if somebody calls and
says, "Oh my my boiler has an issue.
It's got this error code." A good person
that kind of has [clears throat]
experience dealing with this issue. Ah
that error code means this. Have you
tried doing that? That might get you to
fix it right away. The AI that we've
built has that context already built in.
So you can take a picture and it would
recognize, oh, that's the boiler brand.
That's the model. This is the error
code. You might need to bleed the
boiler. Uh that might sound complicated.
Let me guide you to help you to do that
because we can fix it for you right
away. And this just happens from the
get-go. This is a permanent staff member
in your team. You don't need to retrain
people to know this information.
>> The agent, so it's like a WhatsApp
agent. Is that the main line of of
contact kind of?
>> We've got four channels, uh WhatsApp,
SMS, uh email, and phone. And we have
all four because people have different
preferences at different times. What's
good is that they sync together. So you
can start with phone and then pick up an
email and then follow up with SMS.
>> Okay. I think the viewers are going to
be very curious to know how you built
it. How many credits it, you know,
costed?
>> A total of about 2200 2,300 credits. And
for what it's worth, just to to be super
super clear. Since we got into YC, um we
did have to build a lot more on the
engineering side to achieve some of the
functionality we wanted. At the point of
of the application, I think it would
have been closer to 15 to 1,700 prompts
to get to inception of the initial
version, we had customers using the
product and paying and things like that
to try it out. And then after that, we
double down on on building, you know,
these integrations to third party tools
and infrastructure and all of that that
was necessary. And it really was just a
case of once I was able to get the first
few prompts to give me what I wanted. I
think one of the benefits of of not
being technical is I didn't know what
the limitations were. So I just kept
assuming that it could do it and I I I
prompted accordingly. Whereas I think um
if you come from a technical background
and I've spoken to a lot of engineers,
they go in assuming that certain things
are not possible. So they wouldn't
bother trying. I didn't know. So I just
went ahead and and did it which I you
know I think you know one of the best
types of users for level and I'm sure
you've seen this on your side too. It's
often you know product designers,
product [music] managers, non-technical
people that have exposure to engineers
in some way, shape or form I think are
the ones to unlock the kind [music] of
highest level of um of value. Yeah, but
how did you validate the idea? How did
you know uh this was going to work? Or I
guess you maybe you didn't know but you
just went for it. I don't know.
>> This is why I think we went for a
quicker non engineering heavy approach
because the core functionality like the
basics of it you know you can sign up
you can call an agent it would put a
ticket for you in front of you it would
search for you know some of the
maintenance folks [music] that part like
we got it to work and then we just put
it in front of potential customers and
like okay this seems really helpful.
some of then join as paying customers.
Okay, this has legs. This is clearly
something that [music] people are
interested in and they want. That was
the point where we then took a step back
and said, okay, let's start thinking
really about [music] how to build this
in a way where when it has hundreds of
thousands of concurrent users within a
given month, it's built in a way that it
can handle that. But you don't need to
solve a scale problem on day one. If I
built a bad product very well with great
engineering and it had zero validation
when I put it in front of customers, for
me that's a bad outcome. It doesn't
matter that the product was built well.
I'd much rather put together something
that is sufficiently useful, however
quickly hacked together it is. Put it in
front of who you think your customers
are and see if they resonate with the
idea. See if they're interested. See if
they're willing to try it out. Pay for
it ideally because then that gives you
the real signal that uh this is worth
worth building. How long did it take to
make that super early version?
>> I think it must have been about a month,
maybe a month and a half or so of just
like tinkering in the evenings and on
the weekends and things like that to get
it to a stage where it's interesting and
somewhat works. Like a very very early
quick version was like maybe in the
first week, week and a half, but one
where we can put in front of other
people a monthish was about the right
amount of time. And [music] I think what
I was touching on earlier, I'm not sure
if this was um was captured, but it it
is for me I think much more important to
prove that this is something people want
in whatever method you can. And if you
can validate that, then you can spend
some time building it out more
substantially.
>> Let me ask you for some general startup
advice. If you There's probably going to
be a bunch of founders watching this
video and maybe they're thinking about
raising money, maybe bootstrapping,
maybe they don't have an idea yet. Maybe
you do have an idea yet. What are some
quick advice that you have?
>> Well, I see pushes launching quickly as
a main theme because, you know, you find
out as quickly as possible whether
you're on the right track or not. And
also launching doesn't mean you only
need to launch once. You can probably
launch multiple times and people
wouldn't even realize that you'd
launched before. So, how does that
translate into something tangible and
actionable? Um, if you have an idea or a
problem that you think is worth solving,
try and get something that is reasonably
good enough to get into the hands of
people as quickly as possible because
you would be very surprised how wrong
you can be with what you think users
want. But you'll never know until you
speak to them. And if you take a year to
speak to them, you would take a year to
learn that a lot of what you've did
you've done sorry was not worth the
time. Whereas if you get a base level
together within a month or a few weeks,
yeah, you can get that something in
front of them and learn very quickly.
Ah, this part is good. Great. This is
the wrong direction. Let me correct
that. So getting that feedback loop
early. I would would highly recommend
[music] for most people most of the time
if you are building I don't know planes
and rockets probably don't do that. You
might need to spend more time getting it
right before you you know launch. But
you know for the most part with most of
the ideas that people have
>> currently have you moved the project
entirely to like cursor or VS code or
are you still are you collaborating on
lovable?
>> So now the the main use case of lovable
for us is [music] very quickly putting
together UI and wireframing in customer
demo calls especially when they want to
kind of have a look and feel as to how
will this integrate for us or what would
it look like if we had our customers on
this platform and and this is the
workflow. we would use it very quickly
to put something that we can showcase
and when we get that validation we would
then need to integrate it into our
existing tech stack and oftent times
that's kind of beginning to kind of get
away from what was first built unlovable
so I guess it's it role has now shifted
away from what was the core product for
validation and more towards very quickly
putting something together that users
can touch see scroll around play around
with to demonstrate that this is what
it's going to be like what do you think
is that helpful what would you like to
change
>> your go-to market strategy do you have
any advice on that How do you currently
acquire customers, find leads?
>> What we do is two parts. We've gotten
pretty good at LinkedIn marketing. I
think one in four people that we cold
message [music] on LinkedIn reply, uh,
which is, I think, a reasonably high
conversion rate. And then we have a very
good free version of the product that
you can play around with on our homepage
without needing to sign up. So [music]
you can call the agent, email the agent
yourself, WhatsApp the agent, play
around with it. The good thing there is
when someone's tried the product, we
have a rough idea of who they are,
right? We've got the email number now.
And we just reach out to them and say
like, hey, you know, how was your demo?
Did you like it? What didn't you like?
I'd love to actually showcase the
[music] entire product to you if you're
interested in a phone call. Just in the
past 2 weeks, I think we had 100 people
try out the product. And 24 booked demo
calls with [music] us. And I think
that's a number that's going to increase
pretty quickly as, you know, domain
trend rises and more people try out the
product. But it's a great way for us to
actually get in front of people that are
interested in the problem that we're
solving. Do you have any advice uh when
building something with lovable? Right
now we are kind of more discussed like
how to create a successful company kind
of but any specifically lovable advice?
Sometimes I feel like I'm not sure if
it's a good thing, but I feel like I can
write prompts [music] sometimes better
than engineers can because it's a
there's a nontechnical mindset to
writing prompts I found and I think it
works better, which is [music] talk to
it in the least technical way possible
as if a kid could understand. And
sometimes I actually give screenshots of
where I want the change and I describe
the problem and I bullet point what I
want the change to look like and I
[music] typically get my desired outcome
very quickly. I think it's probably
inadvisable to try and change a lot at
the same time. Um, being very very
specific and very focused on your
changes at a time that that's usually
quite helpful. If it's something that's
quite complex and detailed, I'd
sometimes actually go into chat GPT and
I would lazily write what I want and I
would ask chat GPT to frame this in a
way that would be clear and cohesive for
me to put onto lovable. And then chat
GPT would give me the prompt to give to
lovable as well.
>> And do you also brief chat GP that you
want to be involable? Cuz sometimes chat
might be very clear like I'm using
lovable. Um I need a prompt that wants
that's going to achieve the following
outcome. How would you do this? And I'd
read what it says and if I don't like it
or it's too long or it's too, you know,
look, whatever. I would ask it to fix
according. So I'd go back and forth to
chat GPT. In the early days, I do that
to just save money on the level prompts
because it was easy for me to chat to to
chat GPT, get it right, and then go into
lovable to just use one prompt and get
it done. A lot of users I feel like this
is the biggest problem we have that
people get stuck like they start
building something with lovable and then
they just hit like a wall almost. I
think we have made some improvements in
that with the agent and some updates but
[music]
did that ever happen to you and what and
what did you do in those 100%. I think
this is where the um having a background
in product or in design or working with
engineers even if you weren't writing
the code is very helpful because you get
very used to things not working first
time around and needing to spend time to
get it to work. That isn't something
that nontechnical people are used to
when it comes to using the internet.
Like know you write an essay and as you
type you get the words and it [music]
works, right? You're not used to telling
Lovable to do X and it doesn't do X and
you just assume that okay, I guess
Lovable doesn't work. Therefore, I'm
out. But there's a teaching moment there
to kind of get people to understand how
engineering works. And sometimes the
conflict isn't because of something
that's obvious. There's some other file
somewhere else that caused an issue that
you weren't aware of. And in this new
prompt that you've added, those clash
and now there's a new problem. And I
think lovable has improved in trying to
fix things like that. They are, you
know, actively trying to fix issues and
so on. But I think there is a teaching
moment for users to learn why things go
wrong and why that's not always a bad
thing. And writing a prompt to fix what
went wrong is not lovable making a
mistake. Whereas it feels like that
sometimes. One of the suggestions which
an engineer friend of mine recommended
and I did this on my side but they
suggested that if there are certain
things that you know need to exist in
your product build them in early because
you want to get them right payments or
analytics things like that. So, I
probably recommend having like a a
beginner's builder stack for everybody
on lovable. Like, hey, let's just set up
these core things. Like, what are you
building? Okay, great. You're going to
need signup. You're going to need
payments. You're going to need this,
this, that. Press this button. Let's
just get this out of the way because we
don't want these things to break. I was
given that advice. I did that. Those
were the least likely things to break in
in the version of the product I first
built. But I know if I, you know, if
prompt number 30,000 was dealing with
payments, there's I I just expect things
to go wrong.
>> Thank you so much, Isma, for having this
chat and this conversation. I think it
was very valuable for the viewers out
there and we had some really interesting
conversations. You can check out Ismile
on on LinkedIn and Twitter. Thank you so
much Isma and good luck with uh with
everything.
>> Thank you.
